citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
An older zebu cow was noted to have a stiff gait when walking, that the licensee had noticed a few days ago, but had not
contacted the attending veterinarian. Failure to identify and communicate abnormal findings with the veterinarian in a
timely manner, delays appropriate treatment and can cause undue pain and/or distress to the animals. Daily observation
of all animals is necessary to assess their health and well-being. If any problems of animal health, behavior, or well-being
are noted, they must be conveyed to the attending veterinarian in a timely and accurate manner to allow for appropriate
diagnosis and treatment. This cow’s condition must be conveyed to the veterinarian so they can determine the next steps
for diagnosis and a treatment plan.
Correct by: 03/07/2026.

The acquisition and disposition records available for review were not current or complete. The acquisition of animals
obtained (peacocks, rhea, emu, nilgai) and those disposed of (pigs, water buffalo, cows, ostrich, African grey birds,
budgies, barbary sheep, blackbuck, goats) are either missing all or part of the following: dates of acquisition or disposition,
names and addresses of whom the animals were acquired from, USDA license number and/or Driver’s license number.
Other animals (pigs, goats, cows, zebu, and budgies) were born on property but not properly documented with full dates
of birth. Without records which fully disclose the animal’s acquisition as well as disposition information, legal and medical
information traceback is not possible. The facility shall ensure that it makes, keeps, and maintains records which fully and
correctly disclose the following information concerning all exhibition animals: name, address, and USDA number, if
applicable, of the person from whom the animal was acquired from or given to, the date of the acquisition or disposition,
the species and quantity of animals involved. The records shall include any offspring born of any animal while in his or her
possession or under his or her control.
Correct by: 04/15/2026.

There is no documentation maintained to indicate that an annual review of the contingency plan has been completed. The
contingency plan must be reviewed by the facility on at least an annual basis to ensure that it adequately addresses all
required contingency-related criteria and that no updates or changes need to be made to the plan such as changes made
as a result of any recent unforeseen circumstance or the addition of new species. The facility must maintain
documentation of their contingency plan annual reviews including documenting any amendments or changes made since
the previous year's review.
Correct by: 04/15/2026.

Two separate primary indoor enclosures, one housing parakeets, and the other a Blue-fronted Amazon, each contained a
live unprotected electrical cord, accessible to the birds, powering a heat lamp. Each live electrical cord spanned from its
entry point into the primary enclosure for approximately a distance of 2 to 3 feet. Electrical cords pose a significant health
risk to animals that may peck or perch on them. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they
protect the birds from injury.
Corrected at the time of inspection, and to stay corrected from this date, March 2, 2026, forward.

citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, the facility did not provide an adequately detailed species-specific environmental enhancement
plan for all the birds on hand. The facility has acquired ostriches, peacocks, and a rhea, since the last inspection, but they
were not included in the provided enrichment plan. A written plan addressing the specific needs of the varied species is
necessary to ensure that the overall health, well-being, and psychological needs of the birds are being appropriately met.
The facility must develop, document, and follow a species-appropriate plan for environment enhancement adequate to
promote the psychological well-being of the birds. The plan must be approved by the attending veterinarian and must
address social grouping, environmental enrichment, special considerations, restraint devices, and exemptions.
Correct by: 04/15/2026.

citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
An African Grey parrot was singly housed within the main store, adjacent to and across the parking lot from the safari,
unable to see or hear other birds. A special consideration enrichment plan was not specifically implemented or
documented for this individually housed social bird. Without such a plan, this bird may develop abnormal behaviors and
could experience psychological distress. The facility must implement an adequate enrichment plan per the attending
veterinarian’s instructions for all the birds, including special attention regarding enhancement of the environment of those
individually housed social species of birds that are unable to see and hear birds of their own or compatible species.
Correct by 04/15/2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The following indoor primary enclosures needed cleaning to remove excess accumulations of excreta:
• In the African Grey parrot’s primary enclosure, the available perches were mostly covered in a layer of accumulated and
dried excrement, especially around the food and water receptacles. In the tray below the enclosure, was a large
accumulation food waste and excreta.
• In the parakeet aviary, both long wooden perches spanning the width of the aviary had a layer of dried excrement
covering not only most of the top resting surface, but also down the sides of the perches. Excreta and food waste had
further accumulated along the horizontal wood beams that provided part of the frame of the wire enclosure, closest to the
barn walkway.
Regular cleaning is necessary to prevent soiling of the birds and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors.
Excreta, debris, and food waste shall be removed from primary enclosures and from under and around primary
enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulations.
Correct by 03/15/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
n

narrative:

3.153(a)(1)(ii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
Two separate primary indoor enclosures, one housing parakeets, and the other a Blue-fronted Amazon, each contained a

3.154 
   
Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being. 
At the time of inspection, the facility did not provide an adequately detailed species-specific environmental enhancement 
plan for all the birds on hand. The facility has acquired ostriches, peacocks, and a rhea, since the last inspection, but they 
were not included in the provided enrichment plan. A written plan addressing the specific needs of the varied species is

3.154(c)(4) 
   
Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being. 
An African Grey parrot was singly housed within the main store, adjacent to and across the parking lot from the safari,

3.158(a)(1) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The following indoor primary enclosures needed cleaning to remove excess accumulations of excreta: 
• In the African Grey parrot’s primary enclosure, the available perches were mostly covered in a layer of accumulated and 
dried excrement, especially around the food and water receptacles. In the tray below the enclosure, was a large

accumulation food waste and excreta.  
• In the parakeet aviary, both long wooden perches spanning the width of the aviary had a layer of dried excrement

Regular cleaning is necessary to prevent soiling of the birds and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors. 
Excreta, debris, and food waste shall be removed from primary enclosures and from under and around primary

Correct by 03/15/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
